director.summary: Week 21 (ending 26 May 2026) shows broad-based weakness across all three stores, with total sales down 5.2% YoY at group level. This is NOT a calendar distortion—late May 2025 had no major holidays, making YoY comparison reliable. The weakness spans meat (-5.5% avg), produce (-7.5% avg), and bakery (-4.0% avg), suggesting demand headwinds rather than operational failure. However, patterns differ sharply by store: Skerries remains resilient (-3.6% total), Palmerstown struggles hardest (-6.5%), and Balbriggan faces margin pressure despite holding volume. Beer/wine/spirits down 13.5% group-wide is a red flag—possible weather impact (warm spell reducing spirits demand) or competitor activity. Fresh produce shows catastrophic chilled fruit decline (-21% Palmerstown, -24% Balbriggan), pointing to supply, quality, or seasonality issues rather than pricing.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.summary: Worst performer this week at -6.5% total sales. Meat down 7.7%, produce down 7.2%, and personal care cratering at -16.9%. Margin compression across meat (-3.1pp) and bakery scratch (-weak sell-through). Deli is sole bright spot (+4.8%, +7.9% hot food), suggesting lunchtime commuter trade holding. Commuter footfall may be dampened by school holidays or mid-week work patterns. Own-brand weakness (-4.5%) indicates basket trading down or format shift.

stores.name: Balbriggan
stores.summary: Down 5.5% total sales, but more concerning is produce collapse (-14.8%, worst in group) and deli implosion (-10.6%). Chilled fruit specifically down 23.8%—identical pattern to Palmerstown suggests supply or quality issue, not local. Beer/wine down 11.2%. However, edible grocery +4.8% and non-food +6.6% show some resilience; family basket holding better than Palmerstown. Fresh bakery +15.4% is anomaly—possible category reset or new supplier. Waste at 1.51% (highest) may indicate aging stock or poor forecasting.

stores.name: Skerries
stores.summary: Outperforms peers at -3.6% total, with edible grocery actually +8.1% YoY and chicken serveover +9.4%. Produce stable (-0.7% vs peers at -8%). Chilled fruit is outlier +2.8%, suggesting stronger tourist/affluent demographic or better supply chain. Deli down 19.5% (anomalous), driven by huge delicatessen meats drop (-37.6%)—possible staffing or product issue worth investigating. Beer/wine down 13.8% (in line). YTD data shows Skerries +0.34%, only store tracking flat/positive—momentum builder if sustained.

drivers.text: Skerries edible grocery +8.1% and chicken +9.4% show category strength in right conditions; Balbriggan fresh bakery +15.4% signals successful promotional or supply reset.

Chilled fruit down 21-24% at Palmerstown and Balbriggan in parallel—not store-specific. Likely supply shortage, quality decline, or seasonal shift (berries availability). Impacts margin and basket health.
Beer/wine/spirits down 13-15% group-wide despite no calendar excuse—suggests competitor promotion, warm weather reducing spirits mix, or promotional pullback by head office.

Meat margin compression: Palmerstown -3.1pp, Balbriggan -3.9pp on serveover lines. Beef serveover down 18% Balbriggan, -24% Skerries (labour cost? product quality?). Watch for deli labour cost overrun.
Deli inconsistency: Palmerstown +4.8%, Balbriggan -10.6%, Skerries -19.5%. Skerries deli meats collapse (-37.6%) unexplained. Staff rostering or supply issue to diagnose immediately.

priorities: ROOT CAUSE: Chilled fruit decline—contact HQ supply team to identify if category-wide issue (imports, quality, substitution strategy).
INVESTIGATE: Deli performance collapse at Skerries (delicatessen meats -37.6%) and weak Balbriggan deli (-10.6%). Check staffing, product age, cold chain.
ANALYSE: Beer/wine drop across all stores—is this competitor, weather, or promotional calendar? Align with commercial team.
MARGIN RECOVERY: Meat margin compression especially Balbriggan beef. Review pricing, waste, and labour scheduling vs. previous year.
FORECAST: Produce demand weakness may persist if chilled fruit supply constrained through summer. Prepare promotional contingency.

leaks.title: Chilled fruit category meltdown
leaks.fix: 1. Contact supply chain for root cause (import delays, quality fail, grower shortage). 2. Check Balbriggan/Palmerstown planograms—are facings still allocated to 21%+ YoY down? 3. If supply constrained, shift space to stable lines (regular fruit, organic). 4. Review promotional pricing—test elasticity with 10% discount on next order. 5. Communicate to deli: substitute with fruit platters if fresh fruit shortage confirmed.

leaks.title: Skerries deli meats collapse (-37.6%)
leaks.fix: 1. Audit deli staffing schedule vs. last year—check if hours cut or turnover. 2. Inspect delicatessen counter stock age; sample sliced meats for quality (oxidation, odour). 3. Review category mix—did planogram change? Did premium lines get de-listed? 4. If staffing issue, reinstate or hire temp; if product issue, escalate to supplier QA. 5. Promote deli platters at food-to-go till point to recover footfall.

leaks.title: Meat serveover margin erosion (Balbriggan beef -24%, -7.8pp margin)
leaks.fix: 1. Analyse labour cost per transaction—is deli counter overstaffed on slow shifts? 2. Check waste: are cuts being trimmed too aggressively or dating too conservatively? 3. Review pricing vs. LY—if held flat despite cost inflation, escalate to pricing committee. 4. Bench-mark Palmerstown/Skerries pricing to confirm Balbriggan alignment. 5. Test bundle promotions (e.g., beef + ready-made sides) to drive volume and basket.

leaks.title: Personal care steep decline (Palmerstown -16.9%, Balbriggan -17.9%)
leaks.fix: 1. Cross-check with impulse data (D0024 also down -8.5%, -4.1%)—may indicate broader shrinkflation/OOH. 2. Audit planogram compliance—confirm sizes/ranges facing are current. 3. Contact category manager—any delistings or supplier changes last 6 weeks? 4. Check competitor promotional activity (Dunnes, Tesco) on shampoo/deodorant. 5. Flag to merchandising: personal care needs refresh or promotional support.

leaks.title: Beer/wine/spirits systematic underperformance (-13–15%)
leaks.fix: 1. Pull competitor mystery shop data (Dunnes, Tesco, Lidl) for spirits/beer pricing week of 19–25 May. 2. Check if HQ ran simultaneous promotion elsewhere (e.g., online, cash&carry) cannibalizing store footfall. 3. Review store promotions log—confirm on-shelf pricing matches advertised. 4. Analyse mix: are customers trading down to cider/RTD? Check impulse soft drinks data (also weak). 5. Escalate to commercial: is category facing external demand shock (weather, competitor launch) or internal issue?

urgent.text: DELI SUPPLY AUDIT: Skerries manager to inspect delicatessen meats stock quality, shelf life, and staffing schedule TODAY. If quality fail confirmed, escalate to supply chain; if staffing issue, submit emergency hours request.

CHILLED FRUIT ROOT CAUSE: Finance/Supply to contact HQ within 24 hours re: chilled fruit import status. If supply constrained through summer, request contingency planogram and promotional ceiling for June–August.

today: Palmerstown: Audit personal care facing/range—confirm planogram current, check competitor pricing on deodorant/shampoo.
Balbriggan: Measure deli meats shelf life and waste %—compare to same week last year; request any recent delistings from category manager.
Skerries: Deep-dive deli labour cost—pull shift-by-shift payroll for week 21 vs. week 21 LY; identify if hours cut.
GROUP: Pull beer/wine promotional calendar for week of 19–25 May 2025 and week 21 2026—identify any changes or competitor activity.
GROUP: Request chilled fruit suppliers' SLA compliance report—confirm lead times and quality scores vs. last year.
Palmerstown: Review deli hot food +7.9%—interview deli manager on what's driving success; replicate at Balbriggan/Skerries.

compliance.text: WASTE THRESHOLD: Balbriggan dairy waste at 1.51% (above typical 1.2% benchmark). Audit waste reporting—confirm counts accurate and not masking shrink. Escalate to store manager if trend continues.

SHELF LIFE RISK: Produce and deli margin compression + weak sales may indicate aggressive date management. Confirm all marked-down stock is correctly signed/priced per SuperValu regs. Risk: customer complaint, brand damage.

cross.title: Deli–Produce–Bakery triangle: freshness chain integrity
cross.why: Weak deli (esp. Skerries -19.5%), weak chilled fruit (-21–24%), and soft bakery suggest common root—either supply cold-chain failure, or store environment (chiller temps). If one fails, all three suffer margin & waste.
cross.action: Facilities check: temperature data for deli chiller, produce ambient, and bakery proof box for week of 19–25 May. If any 0.5°C above spec, escalate to maintenance. Parallel: ask deli team re: any comments on produce/fruit condition on delivery.

cross.title: Impulse vs. structured grocery split
cross.why: Impulse (D0024) weak across all stores (-3 to -8.5%), but edible grocery (D0025) mixed: Palmerstown -1.4%, Balbriggan +4.8%, Skerries +8.1%. Suggests either impulse shelving issue (planogram, facing) or basket trading down from impulse to budget edible (own-brand driving Skerries +1.2%).
cross.action: Audit impulse displays (confectionery, crisps, soft drinks) at each store vs. planogram photo. Confirm facings, rotation, and price signage current. Check if competitor Lidl/Aldi impulse endcaps are better positioned. If Skerries edible +8.1% driven by own-brand, test branded tier reduction at other stores.

cross.title: Deli synergy leverage: Palmerstown success model
cross.why: Palmerstown deli +4.8% (hot food +7.9%, sandwiches +34.6%) vs. Balbriggan -10.6% and Skerries -19.5%. Palmerstown's commuter lunchtime play is working; deli manager or team driving incremental sales. Knowledge leakage = missed £6k+ opportunity.
cross.action: 1. Interview Palmerstown deli team: what changed in past 8 weeks (staff, menu, merchandising, pricing)? 2. Document best-practice: sample menu, promotion, labour scheduling. 3. Replicate at Balbriggan (family audiences) and Skerries (tourist season footfall). 4. Escalate to Regional: consider deli manager rotation or secondment of high performer.

cross.title: Own-brand momentum at Skerries (+1.2%) vs. decline elsewhere
cross.why: Skerries own-brand +1.2% sales, Palmerstown -4.5%, Balbriggan -5.0%. Skerries customer base or store environment appears more receptive. May indicate affluent/price-insensitive (premium own-brand) or strong private label penetration strategy locally.
cross.action: Pull own-brand mix by line (D0025, D0026) for Skerries vs. peers. Identify which SKUs outperforming (e.g., signature ranges, organic, premium). Test top 5 Skerries performers at Balbriggan with local promotion. If Skerries has better own-brand display/signage, replicate planogram at other stores.

diagnosis.wrong: Broad-based demand collapse (-5.2% group, -6.5% Palmerstown) masking store-specific operational failures in deli (Skerries -37.6% meats), produce supply (chilled fruit -21–24%), and margin compression (meat -3–4pp). Not a pricing or format issue—transaction counts weak across all stores (hourly data shows -29 to -138 transactions YoY), confirming footfall loss.
diagnosis.why: EXTERNAL: Late May typically warm/dry in Ireland (promotes fresh produce, BBQ, soft drinks)—yet produce DOWN and beer/wine DOWN, suggesting either (a) supply shortage masking demand, (b) competitor major promotion or opening nearby, or (c) calendar shift in customer behaviour (e.g., school holiday shopping earlier/online). INTERNAL: Deli margin erosion and staffing gaps (Skerries meats collapse, Balbriggan deli -10.6%) point to rostering cuts or labour cost controls post-budget. Chilled fruit decline is parallel across Palmerstown and Balbriggan—unlikely coincidence, suggests supply or quality issue not captured in alerts.
diagnosis.fix: IMMEDIATE (Today–48hrs): (1) Skerries deli manager inspect meats stock quality and age; pull 6-week labour cost trend vs. LY. (2) Contact HQ supply re: chilled fruit import/quality status; request week 21 2025 supplier SLA compliance. (3) Pull beer/wine promotional calendar and competitor mystery shop. (4) Palmerstown deli team debrief: document what's working (lunchtime focus, menu, pricing), prepare replication pack. (5) Audit impulse planograms vs. standard—check Skerries own-brand driving growth vs. impulse weakness. SHORT-TERM (Week of 27 May): (1) Implement chilled fruit contingency—test promotional discount 10% if supply adequate, or shift space if constrained. (2) Deli labour productivity reset: Balbriggan/Skerries align hours to Palmerstown lunchtime peak model. (3) Beef margin recovery: review pricing vs. competitors; trial bundle (beef + sides) to drive volume. (4) Personal care refresh: coordinate with category manager on delistings, test promotional tier. (5) Beer/wine: confirm no internal pricing/promo conflict; escalate to commercial if external competitor action confirmed.

waste.summary: Balbriggan and Skerries stores generated significant waste during the week of 2026-05-26, with combined losses exceeding €1,400. Balbriggan's waste is driven by pet food (Whiskas, €279), bakery items (demi baguettes, €143), and confectionery with poor sell-through (Cadbury Caramel, Charleville Cheddar). Skerries shows acute produce and deli waste, particularly salad bar (€149 on 11 units), fresh stuffing (€45 waste despite 55 retail sales), and black pudding (€54 waste against only 4 retail units). Palmerstown provided no data for analysis.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.category_breakdown: Unable to assess - no waste data provided for this store.
stores.action: URGENT: Obtain waste data for Palmerstown (356) to complete waste analysis and identify store-specific ordering issues.

stores.name: Balbriggan

top_offenders.insight: Critical ordering problem - 114 units wasted with zero retail sales indicates severe overstock of pet food. No markdown activity suggests product not positioned or promoted. Reduce order quantities immediately and review shelf allocation.

High bakery waste (27% waste rate) despite decent retail sales of 132 units. Suggests over-ordering relative to demand patterns. Review daily production quantities and consider shorter shelf-life planning for bakery items.
Extreme ordering failure - 21 units wasted vs. only 1 retail sale. Confectionery with poor demand. Remove from standard order or reduce to single-unit replenishment until demand stabilizes.

Specialty cheese with 10:1 waste-to-sales ratio indicates poor shelf placement, low visibility or wrong customer profile. Consider discontinuation or trial smaller pack sizes with promotional markdown.
100% waste rate with retail sales equal to waste suggests excessive opening of multi-packs or short shelf-life issues. Monitor display and rotation closely; consider reducing case order depth.

stores.category_breakdown: Pet food and confectionery dominate waste (€361.50 combined), followed by bakery (€142.80) and specialty groceries. Pet food waste is critical - suggests wrong category stocking approach. Confectionery waste points to poor demand forecasting or shelf management in impulse categories. Bakery waste typical but controllable through production scheduling.
stores.action: Immediately halt standard ordering of Whiskas pet food and implement daily-only replenishment based on actual sales. Audit Cadbury Caramel and Charleville Cheddar SKU placement and customer demand - consider delisting if not core to store format.

stores.name: Skerries

top_offenders.insight: CRITICAL: €149 waste on 11 units with only 1.4 retail sales is the highest-value waste item in group. Produce/fresh category operating at catastrophic loss. Immediately reduce daily prep quantities, review shelf-life, or consider outsourced salad bar service.

9:1 waste-to-sales ratio on meat/deli specialty item. Black pudding has poor demand at this location. Reduce order to 2-3 units weekly and test pricing/placement adjustments before full discontinuation.

Bakery/fresh category with 27% waste rate despite 55 retail sales and 15 reductions. Shows markdown dependency - customers only buy when reduced. Consider lower initial price point or reduce order quantities to align with unmarkdowned demand only.

Produce category with strong sales (886 units) but 3% waste and heavy reduction dependency (28.87 units). Indicates ripening/quality issues rather than demand problem. Review supply chain temperature control and delivery frequency.
Premium meat product with 11:1 waste-to-sales ratio. Suggests wrong pack size or customer profile mismatch at this store. Trial smaller 300-400g packs or remove from standard order.

stores.category_breakdown: Produce and fresh items dominate waste (€245+ including salad bar, bananas, herbs), followed by meat/deli (€160+ including black pudding, bacon, ham products), and bakery/fresh prepared (€87). Skerries shows pattern of over-ordering fresh/perishable items with poor demand forecasting. Produce category particularly problematic with high waste despite good sales volume.
stores.action: Immediately scale back salad bar daily preparation - target 50% of current volume and test demand response. Audit all fresh prepared items (stuffing, salads, deli meats) for shelf-life and reorder points. Implement daily ordering for produce to reduce spoilage, particularly bananas showing 3% waste rate.

group_actions: Implement store-level waste tracking in Palmerstown immediately to identify ordering failures; current data gap prevents group-wide benchmarking.

Audit and realign SKU ordering for pet food (Balbriggan), specialty cheeses, and premium meats across all stores - high waste suggests wrong customer format assumptions or poor shelf placement rather than demand.

Review fresh/produce supply chain: Skerries and Balbriggan show patterns of over-preparation and over-ordering of perishables. Introduce daily ordering/preparation for salad bar, bakery, and fresh cut items rather than bulk weekly prep.

Establish markdown dependency audit - flag items like Fresh Stuffing and Bananas where reduction qty is high; implement testing of lower entry prices vs. deeper markdowns to reduce total waste/reduction cost.

Pilot SKU rationalization for poor-performing specialty items (Cadbury Caramel, Charleville Cheddar, Denny Black Pudding, Smoked Bacon Eye Loin) - consider delisting or pack-size changes at stores showing <5 units/week demand.
